question:
B-5. Show that \( 1+(n / 1!)+\left(n^{2} / 2!\right)+\cdots+\left(n^{n} / n!\right)>e^{n} / 2 \) for every integer \( n \geqq 0 \).
Remarks You may assume as known Taylor's remainder formula:
\[
e^{x}-\sum_{k=0}^{n} \frac{x^{k}}{k!}=\frac{1}{n!} \int_{0}^{x}(x-t)^{n} e^{\prime} d t
\]
as well as the fact that
\[
n!=\int_{0}^{-} t^{n} e^{-t} d t
\]
solution:
B-5.
We want to show that
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{n} \frac{n^{k}}{k!}=e^{n}-\frac{1}{n!} \int_{0}^{n}(n-t)^{n} e^{t} d t>\frac{e^{n}}{2}
\]
or, equivalently, that
\[
\begin{array}{l}
n!>2 e^{-n} \int_{0}^{n}(n-t)^{n} e^{t} d t \\
\int_{0}^{\infty} t^{n} e^{-t} d t>2 e^{-n} \int_{0}^{n}(n-t)^{n} e^{t} d t .
\end{array}
\]

Letting \( u=n-t \), this can be transformed into
\[
\int_{0}^{\infty} t^{n} e^{-t} d t>2 \int_{0}^{n} u^{n} e^{-u} d u
\]
which is equivalent to
\[
\int_{n}^{x} u^{n} e^{-u} d u>\int_{0}^{n} u^{n} e^{-u} d u .
\]

Let \( f(u)=u^{n} e^{-u} \). Then it suffices to show that
\[
f(n+h) \geqq f(n-h) \quad \text { for } \quad 0 \leqq h \leqq n .
\]

This is equivalent to
\[
\begin{array}{l}
(n+h)^{n} e^{-n} \geqq(n-h)^{n} e^{n} . \\
n \ln (n+h)-h \geqq n \ln (n-h)+h .
\end{array}
\]

Let \( g(h)=n \ln (n+h)-n \ln (n-h)-2 h \). Then \( g(0)=0 \) and
\[
\frac{d g}{d h}=\frac{n}{n+h}+\frac{n}{n-h}-2=\frac{2 n^{2}}{n^{2}-h^{2}}-2>0
\]
for \( 0<h<n \). Hence \( g(h)>0 \) for \( 0<h<n \). The desired result follows.

descriptive_long.question:
B-5. Show that \( 1+(fixedint / 1!)+\left(fixedint^{2} / 2!\right)+\cdots+\left(fixedint^{fixedint} / fixedint!\right)>e^{fixedint} / 2 \) for every integer \( fixedint \geqq 0 \).
Remarks You may assume as known Taylor's remainder formula:
\[
 e^{variablex}-\sum_{indexvar=0}^{fixedint} \frac{variablex^{indexvar}}{indexvar!}=\frac{1}{fixedint!} \int_{0}^{variablex}(variablex-timevar)^{fixedint} e^{\prime} d timevar
\]
as well as the fact that
\[
 fixedint!=\int_{0}^{-} timevar^{fixedint} e^{-timevar} d timevar
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
B-5.
We want to show that
\[
\sum_{indexvar=0}^{fixedint} \frac{fixedint^{indexvar}}{indexvar!}=e^{fixedint}-\frac{1}{fixedint!} \int_{0}^{fixedint}(fixedint-timevar)^{fixedint} e^{timevar} d timevar>\frac{e^{fixedint}}{2}
\]
or, equivalently, that
\[
\begin{array}{l}
fixedint!>2 e^{-fixedint} \int_{0}^{fixedint}(fixedint-timevar)^{fixedint} e^{timevar} d timevar \\
\int_{0}^{\infty} timevar^{fixedint} e^{-timevar} d timevar>2 e^{-fixedint} \int_{0}^{fixedint}(fixedint-timevar)^{fixedint} e^{timevar} d timevar .
\end{array}
\]

Letting \( shiftvar=fixedint-timevar \), this can be transformed into
\[
\int_{0}^{\infty} timevar^{fixedint} e^{-timevar} d timevar>2 \int_{0}^{fixedint} shiftvar^{fixedint} e^{-shiftvar} d shiftvar
\]
which is equivalent to
\[
\int_{fixedint}^{variablex} shiftvar^{fixedint} e^{-shiftvar} d shiftvar>\int_{0}^{fixedint} shiftvar^{fixedint} e^{-shiftvar} d shiftvar .
\]

Let \( densityfn(shiftvar)=shiftvar^{fixedint} e^{-shiftvar} \). Then it suffices to show that
\[
 densityfn(fixedint+offsetvar) \geqq densityfn(fixedint-offsetvar) \quad \text { for } \quad 0 \leqq offsetvar \leqq fixedint .
\]

This is equivalent to
\[
\begin{array}{l}
(fixedint+offsetvar)^{fixedint} e^{-fixedint} \geqq(fixedint-offsetvar)^{fixedint} e^{fixedint} . \\
fixedint \ln (fixedint+offsetvar)-offsetvar \geqq fixedint \ln (fixedint-offsetvar)+offsetvar .
\end{array}
\]

Let \( growthfn(offsetvar)=fixedint \ln (fixedint+offsetvar)-fixedint \ln (fixedint-offsetvar)-2 offsetvar \). Then \( growthfn(0)=0 \) and
\[
\frac{d growthfn}{d offsetvar}=\frac{fixedint}{fixedint+offsetvar}+\frac{fixedint}{fixedint-offsetvar}-2=\frac{2 fixedint^{2}}{fixedint^{2}-offsetvar^{2}}-2>0
\]
for \( 0<offsetvar<fixedint \). Hence \( growthfn(offsetvar)>0 \) for \( 0<offsetvar<fixedint \). The desired result follows.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
B-5. Show that \( 1+(blueberry / 1!)+\left(blueberry^{2} / 2!\right)+\cdots+\left(blueberry^{blueberry} / blueberry!\right)>e^{blueberry} / 2 \) for every integer \( blueberry \geqq 0 \).
Remarks You may assume as known Taylor's remainder formula:
\[
e^{paperclip}-\sum_{turntable=0}^{blueberry} \frac{paperclip^{turntable}}{turntable!}=\frac{1}{blueberry!} \int_{0}^{paperclip}(paperclip-goldfish)^{blueberry} e^{\prime} d goldfish
\]
as well as the fact that
\[
blueberry!=\int_{0}^{-} goldfish^{blueberry} e^{-goldfish} d goldfish
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
B-5.
We want to show that
\[
\sum_{turntable=0}^{blueberry} \frac{blueberry^{turntable}}{turntable!}=e^{blueberry}-\frac{1}{blueberry!} \int_{0}^{blueberry}(blueberry-goldfish)^{blueberry} e^{goldfish} d goldfish>\frac{e^{blueberry}}{2}
\]
or, equivalently, that
\[
\begin{array}{l}
blueberry!>2 e^{-blueberry} \int_{0}^{blueberry}(blueberry-goldfish)^{blueberry} e^{goldfish} d goldfish \\
\int_{0}^{\infty} goldfish^{blueberry} e^{-goldfish} d goldfish>2 e^{-blueberry} \int_{0}^{blueberry}(blueberry-goldfish)^{blueberry} e^{goldfish} d goldfish .
\end{array}
\]

Letting \( horsehair=blueberry-goldfish \), this can be transformed into
\[
\int_{0}^{\infty} goldfish^{blueberry} e^{-goldfish} d goldfish>2 \int_{0}^{blueberry} horsehair^{blueberry} e^{-horsehair} d horsehair
\]
which is equivalent to
\[
\int_{blueberry}^{paperclip} horsehair^{blueberry} e^{-horsehair} d horsehair>\int_{0}^{blueberry} horsehair^{blueberry} e^{-horsehair} d horsehair .
\]

Let \( chandelier(horsehair)=horsehair^{blueberry} e^{-horsehair} \). Then it suffices to show that
\[
chandelier(blueberry+toothpick) \geqq chandelier(blueberry-toothpick) \quad \text { for } \quad 0 \leqq toothpick \leqq blueberry .
\]

This is equivalent to
\[
\begin{array}{l}
(blueberry+toothpick)^{blueberry} e^{-blueberry} \geqq(blueberry-toothpick)^{blueberry} e^{blueberry} . \\
blueberry \ln (blueberry+toothpick)-toothpick \geqq blueberry \ln (blueberry-toothpick)+toothpick .
\end{array}
\]

Let \( sandstorm(toothpick)=blueberry \ln (blueberry+toothpick)-blueberry \ln (blueberry-toothpick)-2 toothpick \). Then \( sandstorm(0)=0 \) and
\[
\frac{d sandstorm}{d toothpick}=\frac{blueberry}{blueberry+toothpick}+\frac{blueberry}{blueberry-toothpick}-2=\frac{2 blueberry^{2}}{blueberry^{2}-toothpick^{2}}-2>0
\]
for \( 0<toothpick<blueberry \). Hence \( sandstorm(toothpick)>0 \) for \( 0<toothpick<blueberry \). The desired result follows.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
B-5. Show that \( 1+(\zerovalue / 1!)+\left(\zerovalue^{2} / 2!\right)+\cdots+\left(\zerovalue^{\zerovalue} / \zerovalue!\right)>e^{\zerovalue} / 2 \) for every integer \( \zerovalue \geqq 0 \).
Remarks You may assume as known Taylor's remainder formula:
\[
e^{constantvalue}-\sum_{totalcount=0}^{\zerovalue} \frac{constantvalue^{totalcount}}{totalcount!}=\frac{1}{\zerovalue!} \int_{0}^{constantvalue}(constantvalue-staticpoint)^{\zerovalue} e^{\prime} d staticpoint
\]
as well as the fact that
\[
\zerovalue!=\int_{0}^{-} staticpoint^{\zerovalue} e^{-staticpoint} d staticpoint
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
B-5.
We want to show that
\[
\sum_{totalcount=0}^{\zerovalue} \frac{\zerovalue^{totalcount}}{totalcount!}=e^{\zerovalue}-\frac{1}{\zerovalue!} \int_{0}^{\zerovalue}(\zerovalue-staticpoint)^{\zerovalue} e^{staticpoint} d staticpoint>\frac{e^{\zerovalue}}{2}
\]
or, equivalently, that
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\zerovalue!>2 e^{-\zerovalue} \int_{0}^{\zerovalue}(\zerovalue-staticpoint)^{\zerovalue} e^{staticpoint} d staticpoint \\
\int_{0}^{\infty} staticpoint^{\zerovalue} e^{-staticpoint} d staticpoint>2 e^{-\zerovalue} \int_{0}^{\zerovalue} baselinepoint^{\zerovalue} e^{-baselinepoint} d baselinepoint .
\end{array}
\]

Letting \( baselinepoint=\zerovalue-staticpoint \), this can be transformed into
\[
\int_{0}^{\infty} staticpoint^{\zerovalue} e^{-staticpoint} d staticpoint>2 \int_{0}^{\zerovalue} baselinepoint^{\zerovalue} e^{-baselinepoint} d baselinepoint
\]
which is equivalent to
\[
\int_{\zerovalue}^{constantvalue} baselinepoint^{\zerovalue} e^{-baselinepoint} d baselinepoint>\int_{0}^{\zerovalue} baselinepoint^{\zerovalue} e^{-baselinepoint} d baselinepoint .
\]

Let \( staticnumber(baselinepoint)=baselinepoint^{\zerovalue} e^{-baselinepoint} \). Then it suffices to show that
\[
staticnumber(\zerovalue+unchanged) \geqq staticnumber(\zerovalue-unchanged) \quad \text { for } \quad 0 \leqq unchanged \leqq \zerovalue .
\]

This is equivalent to
\[
\begin{array}{l}
(\zerovalue+unchanged)^{\zerovalue} e^{-\zerovalue} \geqq(\zerovalue-unchanged)^{\zerovalue} e^{\zerovalue} . \\
\zerovalue \ln (\zerovalue+unchanged)-unchanged \geqq \zerovalue \ln (\zerovalue-unchanged)+unchanged .
\end{array}
\]

Let \( constantstate(unchanged)=\zerovalue \ln (\zerovalue+unchanged)-\zerovalue \ln (\zerovalue-unchanged)-2 unchanged \). Then \( constantstate(0)=0 \) and
\[
\frac{d constantstate}{d unchanged}=\frac{\zerovalue}{\zerovalue+unchanged}+\frac{\zerovalue}{\zerovalue-unchanged}-2=\frac{2 \zerovalue^{2}}{\zerovalue^{2}-unchanged^{2}}-2>0
\]
for \( 0<unchanged<\zerovalue \). Hence \( constantstate(unchanged)>0 \) for \( 0<unchanged<\zerovalue \). The desired result follows.

garbled_string.question:
B-5. Show that \( 1+(asdfghjk / 1!)+\left(asdfghjk^{2} / 2!\right)+\cdots+\left(asdfghjk^{asdfghjk} / asdfghjk!\right)>e^{asdfghjk} / 2 \) for every integer \( asdfghjk \geqq 0 \).
Remarks You may assume as known Taylor's remainder formula:
\[
e^{qzxwvtnp}-\sum_{lmnoprqs=0}^{asdfghjk} \frac{qzxwvtnp^{lmnoprqs}}{lmnoprqs!}=\frac{1}{asdfghjk!} \int_{0}^{qzxwvtnp}(qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla)^{asdfghjk} e^{\prime} d hjgrksla
\]
as well as the fact that
\[
asdfghjk!=\int_{0}^{-} hjgrksla^{asdfghjk} e^{-hjgrksla} d hjgrksla
\]
garbled_string.solution:
B-5.
We want to show that
\[
\sum_{lmnoprqs=0}^{asdfghjk} \frac{asdfghjk^{lmnoprqs}}{lmnoprqs!}=e^{asdfghjk}-\frac{1}{asdfghjk!} \int_{0}^{asdfghjk}(asdfghjk-hjgrksla)^{asdfghjk} e^{hjgrksla} d hjgrksla>\frac{e^{asdfghjk}}{2}
\]
or, equivalently, that
\[
\begin{array}{l}
asdfghjk!>2 e^{-asdfghjk} \int_{0}^{asdfghjk}(asdfghjk-hjgrksla)^{asdfghjk} e^{hjgrksla} d hjgrksla \\
\int_{0}^{\infty} hjgrksla^{asdfghjk} e^{-hjgrksla} d hjgrksla>2 e^{-asdfghjk} \int_{0}^{asdfghjk}(asdfghjk-hjgrksla)^{asdfghjk} e^{hjgrksla} d hjgrksla .
\end{array}
\]

Letting \( vcbnerpm=asdfghjk-hjgrksla \), this can be transformed into
\[
\int_{0}^{\infty} hjgrksla^{asdfghjk} e^{-hjgrksla} d hjgrksla>2 \int_{0}^{asdfghjk} vcbnerpm^{asdfghjk} e^{-vcbnerpm} d vcbnerpm
\]
which is equivalent to
\[
\int_{asdfghjk}^{qzxwvtnp} vcbnerpm^{asdfghjk} e^{-vcbnerpm} d vcbnerpm>\int_{0}^{asdfghjk} vcbnerpm^{asdfghjk} e^{-vcbnerpm} d vcbnerpm .
\]

Let \( zxcvbnml(vcbnerpm)=vcbnerpm^{asdfghjk} e^{-vcbnerpm} \). Then it suffices to show that
\[
zxcvbnml(asdfghjk+sdfkqwer) \geqq zxcvbnml(asdfghjk-sdfkqwer) \quad \text { for } \quad 0 \leqq sdfkqwer \leqq asdfghjk .
\]

This is equivalent to
\[
\begin{array}{l}
(asdfghjk+sdfkqwer)^{asdfghjk} e^{-asdfghjk} \geqq(asdfghjk-sdfkqwer)^{asdfghjk} e^{asdfghjk} . \\
asdfghjk \ln (asdfghjk+sdfkqwer)-sdfkqwer \geqq asdfghjk \ln (asdfghjk-sdfkqwer)+sdfkqwer .
\end{array}
\]

Let \( pokiuytr(sdfkqwer)=asdfghjk \ln (asdfghjk+sdfkqwer)-asdfghjk \ln (asdfghjk-sdfkqwer)-2 sdfkqwer \). Then \( pokiuytr(0)=0 \) and
\[
\frac{d pokiuytr}{d sdfkqwer}=\frac{asdfghjk}{asdfghjk+sdfkqwer}+\frac{asdfghjk}{asdfghjk-sdfkqwer}-2=\frac{2 asdfghjk^{2}}{asdfghjk^{2}-sdfkqwer^{2}}-2>0
\]
for \( 0<sdfkqwer<asdfghjk \). Hence \( pokiuytr(sdfkqwer)>0 \) for \( 0<sdfkqwer<asdfghjk \). The desired result follows.

kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\[
S_{n}:=\sum_{k=0}^{\,n}\frac{n^{k}}{k!},\qquad  
P_{n}:=e^{-n}S_{n}=\mathbb P\!\left\{X_{n}\le n\right\},
\qquad X_{n}\sim\text{\rm Poisson}(n),\qquad n\in\mathbb N .
\]

(a)  Prove that the sequence $\bigl(P_{n}\bigr)_{n\ge 0}$ is strictly decreasing and that  
\[
\lim_{n\to\infty}P_{n}=\frac12 .
\]

(b)  Show the quantitative two-sided bound  
\[
\boxed{\;
\frac12+\frac{1}{7\sqrt n}
\;<\;
P_{n}
\;<\;
\frac12+\frac{1}{2\sqrt n}
\;}\qquad(n\ge 3).
\]

(c)  Deduce the sharpened estimate  
\[
S_{n}>e^{n}\!
\left(
\frac12+\frac{1}{7\sqrt n}
\right)
\qquad(n\ge 3).
\]

You may use without proof  

(i)  the Gamma-integral $\Gamma(m+1)=\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty}t^{m}e^{-t}\,dt$,  

(ii) Stirling's two-sided estimate  
\[
\sqrt{2\pi}\,m^{\,m+\frac12}e^{-m}
<
m!
<
\sqrt{2\pi}\,m^{\,m+\frac12}e^{-m}\exp\!\bigl(\tfrac{1}{12m}\bigr)
\qquad(m\ge 1),
\]

(iii) the fact that the Poisson probability mass function is unimodal, its maximum being attained at $k=n$ (and also at $k=n-1$ when $n\ge1$).

\bigskip
kernel_variant.solution:
\textbf{Step 0.  Probabilistic reformulation}

For $X_{n}\sim\text{\rm Poisson}(n)$,
\[
P_{n}=e^{-n}\sum_{k=0}^{n}\frac{n^{k}}{k!}
      =\mathbb P\!\bigl\{X_{n}\le n\bigr\},
\qquad
S_{n}=e^{n}P_{n}.
\]

\bigskip
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\textbf{Step 1.  A \emph{correct} integral representation}

Denote by  
\[
\Gamma(s,x)=\int_{x}^{\infty}t^{s-1}e^{-t}\,dt\qquad(s>0,\;x\ge0)
\]
the \emph{upper} incomplete Gamma function.  
Since $\Gamma(n+1)=n!$, changing the dummy letter gives  
\[
P_{n}
  =e^{-n}\sum_{k=0}^{n}\frac{n^{k}}{k!}
  =\frac{\Gamma(n+1,n)}{n!}
  =\frac{1}{n!}\int_{n}^{\infty}t^{n}e^{-t}\,dt.
\tag{1}
\]

\bigskip
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\textbf{Part (a).  Strict decrease and limit}

\emph{(i)  Strict decrease.}
Using (1) for $n$ and $n+1$ we obtain
\[
P_{n}-P_{n+1}
   =\frac{1}{n!}\!\int_{n}^{\infty}\!t^{n}e^{-t}\,dt
    -\frac{1}{(n+1)!}\!\int_{\,n+1}^{\infty}\!t^{\,n+1}e^{-t}\,dt
   =\frac{1}{(n+1)!}\Bigl[I_{n}-n^{\,n+1}e^{-n}\Bigr],
\]
where  
\[
I_{n}:=\int_{n}^{\,n+1}t^{\,n+1}e^{-t}\,dt.
\tag{2}
\]

\emph{Claim.}  For every $n\ge0$ one has $I_{n}>n^{\,n+1}e^{-n}$.

\emph{Proof of the claim.}
Put $t=n+s$ with $0\le s\le1$; then
\[
I_{n}=n^{\,n+1}e^{-n}\!\int_{0}^{1}
          \Bigl(1+\frac{s}{n}\Bigr)^{n+1}e^{-s}\,ds.
\]
Define  
\[
f_{n}(s):=\Bigl(1+\frac{s}{n}\Bigr)^{n+1}e^{-s},\qquad 0\le s\le1 .
\]
A simple derivative computation gives
\[
f_{n}'(s)=f_{n}(s)\,\frac{1-s}{n+s}\;>\;0
\qquad(0<s<1),
\]
so $f_{n}$ is \emph{strictly increasing} on $[0,1]$ and $f_{n}(0)=1$.  
Hence $f_{n}(s)>1$ for $0<s\le1$, and
\[
I_{n}=n^{\,n+1}e^{-n}\!\int_{0}^{1}f_{n}(s)\,ds
     >n^{\,n+1}e^{-n}\!\int_{0}^{1}1\,ds
     =n^{\,n+1}e^{-n}.
\]
The claim is proved.

Because the front factor $1/(n+1)!$ is positive, the bracket in (2) is
positive, and
\[
P_{n}>P_{n+1}\qquad(n\ge0).
\]
Strict decrease is now established.

\smallskip
\emph{(ii)  The limit.}
Standardising $X_{n}$,
\[
Z_{n}:=\frac{X_{n}-n}{\sqrt n},
\]
the classical Central Limit Theorem yields $Z_{n}\Longrightarrow N(0,1)$.
Since $P_{n}=\mathbb P\{Z_{n}\le0\}$ and the Gaussian limit is symmetric,
\[
\lim_{n\to\infty}P_{n}=\Phi(0)=\frac12.
\]

\bigskip
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\textbf{Part (b).  A two-sided $\sqrt n$-law}

Set
\[
p_{n}:=\mathbb P\!\bigl\{X_{n}=n\bigr\}
      =e^{-n}\frac{n^{n}}{n!},\qquad
a_{n}:=\mathbb P\!\bigl\{X_{n}\le n-1\bigr\},\qquad
c_{n}:=\mathbb P\!\bigl\{X_{n}\ge n+1\bigr\},
\]
so that
\[
P_{n}=a_{n}+p_{n},\qquad
1=a_{n}+p_{n}+c_{n}.
\]

\medskip
\emph{Lemma 1 (right tail dominates the far left).}
For every $n\ge1$ and $h\ge0$,
\[
\mathbb P\!\bigl\{X_{n}=n+h\bigr\}\;\ge\;
\mathbb P\!\bigl\{X_{n}=n-1-h\bigr\}.
\tag{3}
\]

\emph{Proof.}
Using the explicit density,
\[
R_{h}:=
\frac{\mathbb P\{X_{n}=n+h\}}{\mathbb P\{X_{n}=n-1-h\}}
  =n^{\,2h+1}\frac{(n-1-h)!}{(n+h)!}
  =\prod_{j=0}^{2h}\frac{n}{n-h+j}.
\]
Pair the symmetric factors:
\[
(n-h+j)\,(n+h-j)\le n^{2}\qquad(0\le j\le h),
\]
so each product of two consecutive denominators does not exceed $n^{2}$
and therefore the whole denominator does not exceed the numerator
$n^{\,2h+1}$.  
Hence $R_{h}\ge1$, proving (3).

Summing (3) over $h\ge0$ gives
\[
p_{n}+c_{n}\;\ge\;a_{n}.
\tag{4}
\]

\smallskip
\emph{Lemma 2 (left side dominates the symmetric right).}
For every $n\ge1$ and $h\ge1$,
\[
\mathbb P\!\bigl\{X_{n}=n-h\bigr\}\;\ge\;
\mathbb P\!\bigl\{X_{n}=n+h\bigr\}.
\tag{5}
\]
\emph{Proof.}
The Poisson mass function is strictly increasing up to $k=n$ and
strictly decreasing afterwards (Fact (iii)), whence (5).\hfill$\square$

Summing (5) over $h\ge1$ yields
\[
a_{n}\;\ge\;c_{n}.
\tag{6}
\]

\smallskip
\emph{Consequences of the two lemmas.}
From (4) we get $a_{n}\le p_{n}+c_{n}$, while (6) implies $c_{n}\le a_{n}$.
Combining,
\[
a_{n}\le\frac12,
\qquad
a_{n}\ge\frac{1-p_{n}}{2}.
\tag{7}
\]
Therefore
\[
\frac12+\frac{p_{n}}{2}\;\le\;P_{n}=a_{n}+p_{n}\;\le\;\frac12+p_{n}.
\tag{8}
\]

\smallskip
\emph{Bounding $p_{n}$.}
By Stirling's estimate (ii), for $n\ge3$,
\[
\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi n}}\!\Bigl(1-\frac{1}{12n}\Bigr)
<p_{n}
<
\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi n}}.
\tag{9}
\]
Because $1/\sqrt{2\pi}\approx0.399<\tfrac12$,
\[
p_{n}<\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi n}}<\frac{1}{2\sqrt n},
\qquad n\ge3.
\tag{10}
\]

\smallskip
\emph{Finishing the two-sided estimate.}
Insert the left inequality of (9) into the left inequality of (8) and the
right inequality of (10) into the right inequality of (8); for $n\ge3$,
\[
\frac12+\frac{1}{2\sqrt{2\pi n}}\Bigl(1-\frac{1}{12n}\Bigr)
<P_{n}
<\frac12+\frac{1}{2\sqrt n}.
\]
Since
\[
\frac{1}{2\sqrt{2\pi}}\Bigl(1-\frac{1}{36}\Bigr)
>\frac17,
\]
the lower bound is stronger than
$\dfrac12+\dfrac{1}{7\sqrt n}$ for $n\ge3$, and part~(b) is proved.

\bigskip
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\textbf{Part (c).  Inequality for $S_{n}$}

Multiplying the lower bound in (b) by $e^{n}$ yields
\[
S_{n}=e^{n}P_{n}
>
e^{n}\!\left(\frac12+\frac{1}{7\sqrt n}\right),
\qquad n\ge 3,
\]
which is the desired estimate.
\hfill$\square$

\bigskip
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Additional concepts.  
   • The problem is now phrased in terms of the regularised incomplete-gamma function and the Poisson distribution, forcing the solver to recognise and exploit these advanced connections.  
   • A central-limit-type estimate with an explicit speed of convergence is required; this entails invoking the Berry–Esseen theorem (or an equivalent quantitative local limit theorem).

2. Extra layers.  
   • Part (a) needs an argument based on the shape of the density t^{n}e^{−t}, going beyond the elementary rearrangement used in the original problem.  
   • Part (b) demands explicit error bounds rather than a mere comparison with a fixed constant such as ½ or ⅓.  Handling the constant and checking its size for all n require non-trivial care.  

3. Greater technical load.  
   • Passing from inequalities with a fixed numerical constant to asymptotically sharp √n-dependent bounds involves deeper probability theory and Stirling-type approximations.  
   • The monotonicity proof, although elementary in principle, now operates at the level of incomplete-gamma integrals rather than simple sums, increasing the analytical sophistication.

4. Overall escalation.  
   The original task was to prove an inequality with a universal constant.  
   The enhanced variant asks for:  
      – monotonicity,  
      – a limiting value,  
      – an explicit O(n^{−½}) error term with a concrete constant,  
   thereby multiplying both the conceptual breadth and the technical depth that the solver must master.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\[
S_{n}:=\sum_{k=0}^{\,n}\frac{n^{k}}{k!},\qquad  
P_{n}:=e^{-n}S_{n}=\mathbb P\!\left\{X_{n}\le n\right\},
\qquad X_{n}\sim\text{\rm Poisson}(n),\qquad n\in\mathbb N .
\]

(a)  Prove that the sequence $\bigl(P_{n}\bigr)_{n\ge 0}$ is strictly decreasing and that  
\[
\lim_{n\to\infty}P_{n}=\frac12 .
\]

(b)  Show the quantitative two-sided bound  
\[
\boxed{\;
\frac12+\frac{1}{7\sqrt n}
\;<\;
P_{n}
\;<\;
\frac12+\frac{1}{2\sqrt n}
\;}\qquad(n\ge 3).
\]

(c)  Deduce the sharpened estimate  
\[
S_{n}>e^{n}\!
\left(
\frac12+\frac{1}{7\sqrt n}
\right)
\qquad(n\ge 3).
\]

You may use without proof  

(i)  the Gamma-integral $\Gamma(m+1)=\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty}t^{m}e^{-t}\,dt$,  

(ii) Stirling's two-sided estimate  
\[
\sqrt{2\pi}\,m^{\,m+\frac12}e^{-m}
<
m!
<
\sqrt{2\pi}\,m^{\,m+\frac12}e^{-m}\exp\!\bigl(\tfrac{1}{12m}\bigr)
\qquad(m\ge 1),
\]

(iii) the fact that the Poisson probability mass function is unimodal, its maximum being attained at $k=n$ (and also at $k=n-1$ when $n\ge1$).

\bigskip
original_kernel_variant.solution:
\textbf{Step 0.  Probabilistic reformulation}

For $X_{n}\sim\text{\rm Poisson}(n)$,
\[
P_{n}=e^{-n}\sum_{k=0}^{n}\frac{n^{k}}{k!}
      =\mathbb P\!\bigl\{X_{n}\le n\bigr\},
\qquad
S_{n}=e^{n}P_{n}.
\]

\bigskip
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\textbf{Step 1.  A \emph{correct} integral representation}

Denote by  
\[
\Gamma(s,x)=\int_{x}^{\infty}t^{s-1}e^{-t}\,dt\qquad(s>0,\;x\ge0)
\]
the \emph{upper} incomplete Gamma function.  
Since $\Gamma(n+1)=n!$, changing the dummy letter gives  
\[
P_{n}
  =e^{-n}\sum_{k=0}^{n}\frac{n^{k}}{k!}
  =\frac{\Gamma(n+1,n)}{n!}
  =\frac{1}{n!}\int_{n}^{\infty}t^{n}e^{-t}\,dt.
\tag{1}
\]

\bigskip
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\textbf{Part (a).  Strict decrease and limit}

\emph{(i)  Strict decrease.}
Using (1) for $n$ and $n+1$ we obtain
\[
P_{n}-P_{n+1}
   =\frac{1}{n!}\!\int_{n}^{\infty}\!t^{n}e^{-t}\,dt
    -\frac{1}{(n+1)!}\!\int_{\,n+1}^{\infty}\!t^{\,n+1}e^{-t}\,dt
   =\frac{1}{(n+1)!}\Bigl[I_{n}-n^{\,n+1}e^{-n}\Bigr],
\]
where  
\[
I_{n}:=\int_{n}^{\,n+1}t^{\,n+1}e^{-t}\,dt.
\tag{2}
\]

\emph{Claim.}  For every $n\ge0$ one has $I_{n}>n^{\,n+1}e^{-n}$.

\emph{Proof of the claim.}
Put $t=n+s$ with $0\le s\le1$; then
\[
I_{n}=n^{\,n+1}e^{-n}\!\int_{0}^{1}
          \Bigl(1+\frac{s}{n}\Bigr)^{n+1}e^{-s}\,ds.
\]
Define  
\[
f_{n}(s):=\Bigl(1+\frac{s}{n}\Bigr)^{n+1}e^{-s},\qquad 0\le s\le1 .
\]
A simple derivative computation gives
\[
f_{n}'(s)=f_{n}(s)\,\frac{1-s}{n+s}\;>\;0
\qquad(0<s<1),
\]
so $f_{n}$ is \emph{strictly increasing} on $[0,1]$ and $f_{n}(0)=1$.  
Hence $f_{n}(s)>1$ for $0<s\le1$, and
\[
I_{n}=n^{\,n+1}e^{-n}\!\int_{0}^{1}f_{n}(s)\,ds
     >n^{\,n+1}e^{-n}\!\int_{0}^{1}1\,ds
     =n^{\,n+1}e^{-n}.
\]
The claim is proved.

Because the front factor $1/(n+1)!$ is positive, the bracket in (2) is
positive, and
\[
P_{n}>P_{n+1}\qquad(n\ge0).
\]
Strict decrease is now established.

\smallskip
\emph{(ii)  The limit.}
Standardising $X_{n}$,
\[
Z_{n}:=\frac{X_{n}-n}{\sqrt n},
\]
the classical Central Limit Theorem yields $Z_{n}\Longrightarrow N(0,1)$.
Since $P_{n}=\mathbb P\{Z_{n}\le0\}$ and the Gaussian limit is symmetric,
\[
\lim_{n\to\infty}P_{n}=\Phi(0)=\frac12.
\]

\bigskip
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\textbf{Part (b).  A two-sided $\sqrt n$-law}

Set
\[
p_{n}:=\mathbb P\!\bigl\{X_{n}=n\bigr\}
      =e^{-n}\frac{n^{n}}{n!},\qquad
a_{n}:=\mathbb P\!\bigl\{X_{n}\le n-1\bigr\},\qquad
c_{n}:=\mathbb P\!\bigl\{X_{n}\ge n+1\bigr\},
\]
so that
\[
P_{n}=a_{n}+p_{n},\qquad
1=a_{n}+p_{n}+c_{n}.
\]

\medskip
\emph{Lemma 1 (right tail dominates the far left).}
For every $n\ge1$ and $h\ge0$,
\[
\mathbb P\!\bigl\{X_{n}=n+h\bigr\}\;\ge\;
\mathbb P\!\bigl\{X_{n}=n-1-h\bigr\}.
\tag{3}
\]

\emph{Proof.}
Using the explicit density,
\[
R_{h}:=
\frac{\mathbb P\{X_{n}=n+h\}}{\mathbb P\{X_{n}=n-1-h\}}
  =n^{\,2h+1}\frac{(n-1-h)!}{(n+h)!}
  =\prod_{j=0}^{2h}\frac{n}{n-h+j}.
\]
Pair the symmetric factors:
\[
(n-h+j)\,(n+h-j)\le n^{2}\qquad(0\le j\le h),
\]
so each product of two consecutive denominators does not exceed $n^{2}$
and therefore the whole denominator does not exceed the numerator
$n^{\,2h+1}$.  
Hence $R_{h}\ge1$, proving (3).

Summing (3) over $h\ge0$ gives
\[
p_{n}+c_{n}\;\ge\;a_{n}.
\tag{4}
\]

\smallskip
\emph{Lemma 2 (left side dominates the symmetric right).}
For every $n\ge1$ and $h\ge1$,
\[
\mathbb P\!\bigl\{X_{n}=n-h\bigr\}\;\ge\;
\mathbb P\!\bigl\{X_{n}=n+h\bigr\}.
\tag{5}
\]
\emph{Proof.}
The Poisson mass function is strictly increasing up to $k=n$ and
strictly decreasing afterwards (Fact (iii)), whence (5).\hfill$\square$

Summing (5) over $h\ge1$ yields
\[
a_{n}\;\ge\;c_{n}.
\tag{6}
\]

\smallskip
\emph{Consequences of the two lemmas.}
From (4) we get $a_{n}\le p_{n}+c_{n}$, while (6) implies $c_{n}\le a_{n}$.
Combining,
\[
a_{n}\le\frac12,
\qquad
a_{n}\ge\frac{1-p_{n}}{2}.
\tag{7}
\]
Therefore
\[
\frac12+\frac{p_{n}}{2}\;\le\;P_{n}=a_{n}+p_{n}\;\le\;\frac12+p_{n}.
\tag{8}
\]

\smallskip
\emph{Bounding $p_{n}$.}
By Stirling's estimate (ii), for $n\ge3$,
\[
\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi n}}\!\Bigl(1-\frac{1}{12n}\Bigr)
<p_{n}
<
\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi n}}.
\tag{9}
\]
Because $1/\sqrt{2\pi}\approx0.399<\tfrac12$,
\[
p_{n}<\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi n}}<\frac{1}{2\sqrt n},
\qquad n\ge3.
\tag{10}
\]

\smallskip
\emph{Finishing the two-sided estimate.}
Insert the left inequality of (9) into the left inequality of (8) and the
right inequality of (10) into the right inequality of (8); for $n\ge3$,
\[
\frac12+\frac{1}{2\sqrt{2\pi n}}\Bigl(1-\frac{1}{12n}\Bigr)
<P_{n}
<\frac12+\frac{1}{2\sqrt n}.
\]
Since
\[
\frac{1}{2\sqrt{2\pi}}\Bigl(1-\frac{1}{36}\Bigr)
>\frac17,
\]
the lower bound is stronger than
$\dfrac12+\dfrac{1}{7\sqrt n}$ for $n\ge3$, and part~(b) is proved.

\bigskip
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\textbf{Part (c).  Inequality for $S_{n}$}

Multiplying the lower bound in (b) by $e^{n}$ yields
\[
S_{n}=e^{n}P_{n}
>
e^{n}\!\left(\frac12+\frac{1}{7\sqrt n}\right),
\qquad n\ge 3,
\]
which is the desired estimate.
\hfill$\square$

\bigskip
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Additional concepts.  
   • The problem is now phrased in terms of the regularised incomplete-gamma function and the Poisson distribution, forcing the solver to recognise and exploit these advanced connections.  
   • A central-limit-type estimate with an explicit speed of convergence is required; this entails invoking the Berry–Esseen theorem (or an equivalent quantitative local limit theorem).

2. Extra layers.  
   • Part (a) needs an argument based on the shape of the density t^{n}e^{−t}, going beyond the elementary rearrangement used in the original problem.  
   • Part (b) demands explicit error bounds rather than a mere comparison with a fixed constant such as ½ or ⅓.  Handling the constant and checking its size for all n require non-trivial care.  

3. Greater technical load.  
   • Passing from inequalities with a fixed numerical constant to asymptotically sharp √n-dependent bounds involves deeper probability theory and Stirling-type approximations.  
   • The monotonicity proof, although elementary in principle, now operates at the level of incomplete-gamma integrals rather than simple sums, increasing the analytical sophistication.

4. Overall escalation.  
   The original task was to prove an inequality with a universal constant.  
   The enhanced variant asks for:  
      – monotonicity,  
      – a limiting value,  
      – an explicit O(n^{−½}) error term with a concrete constant,  
   thereby multiplying both the conceptual breadth and the technical depth that the solver must master.
